research_question: Can an adversarial AI 'devil's advocate' that interrogates the reviewer's justifications improve mid-level reviewers' calibration — the match between their confidence in a review decision and its actual correctness — on legacy code where automation bias is the dominant failure mode?
intervention_primitive: a challenger agent that, after a reviewer writes a comment or approves a hunk, surfaces counterexamples, asks 'what would have to be true elsewhere in the repo for this to be safe?', and forces the reviewer to respond before submission; compared against a standard suggestion-style AI reviewer and against no tool
one_sentence_claim: Reviewers paired with an adversarial challenger show tighter confidence-accuracy calibration on legacy PRs than those using a suggestion-style AI, even when raw defect-catch rates are similar.
why_divergent: Differs from branch A on human_system_relationship (adversarial_check vs system_augments_human) and method_family (generative design of a novel interaction vs evaluative comparison on a fixed stimulus set); differs from branch C on intervention_primitive (a challenger agent vs an onboarding scaffold) and research_question (calibration vs learning trajectory).

grounding.quoted_claim: AI decision-support can erode domain-specific autonomy through absent failure indicators and unconscious value shifts, motivating design patterns such as defeater mechanisms and reflective-practice support to preserve skilled competence.

Agency cues in digital systems can invoke a machine heuristic that alters perceived trustworthiness independently of actual source quality, meaning structural interface features can bias credibility judgments in ways users do not consciously notice.

An LLM-agent-based simulation of the review process modelling distinct reviewer roles reveals that simulated reviewer biases can account for substantial variation in review outcomes, demonstrating the tractability of agent-mediated review research.

LLM-generated feedback can complement but not replace human expert review, with overlap between AI and human reviewers comparable to human-human overlap, suggesting AI review tools occupy a supporting rather than authoritative role.
